coredata.dc:title: Optimized ANN for LiFePO4 battery charge estimation using principal components based feature generation
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd on behalf of Beijing Institute of Technology Press Co., Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd on behalf of Beijing Institute of Technology Press Co., Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Mehta, Chaitali
Sant, Amit V.
Sharma, Paawan
coredata.dc:description: Electric vehicles (EVs) have gained prominence in the present energy transition scenario. Widespread adoption of EVs necessitates an accurate State of Charge estimation (SoC) algorithm. Integrating predictive SoC estimations with smart charging strategies not only optimizes charging efficiency and grid reliability but also extends battery lifespan while continuously enhancing the accuracy of SoC predictions, marking a crucial milestone in sustainable electric vehicle technology. In this research study, machine learning methods, particularly Artificial Neural Networks (ANN), are employed for SoC estimation of LiFePO4 batteries, resulting in efficient and accurate estimation algorithms. The investigation first focuses on developing a custom-designed battery pack with 12 ​V, 4 Ah capacity with a facility for real-time data collection through a dedicated hardware setup. The voltage, current and open-circuit voltage of the battery are monitored with computerized battery analyzer. The battery temperature is sensed with a DHT22 temperature sensor interfaced with Raspberry Pi. Principal components are derived for the collected battery data set and analyzed for feature engineering. Three principal components were generated as input parameters for the developed ANN. Early Stopping for the ANN was also implemented to achieve faster convergence of the ANN. While considering eleven combinations for ten different optimizers loss function is minimized. Comparative analysis of hyperparameter tuning and optimizer selection revealed that the Adafactor optimizer with specific settings produced the best results with an RMSE value of 0.4083 and an R2 Score of 0.9998. The proposed algorithm was also implemented for two different types of datasets, a UDDS drive cycle and a standard cell-level dataset. The results obtained were in line with the results obtained with the ANN model developed based on the data collected from the developed experimental setup.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Battery
Feature engineering
Principal component analysis
Artificial neural networks
Optimizers

Performance analysis of the proposed algorithm with the dataset for standard cell-level dataset

(a)
V batt
vs
O C V
relation for discharge current 0.5 ​A. (b)
V batt
vs
O C V
relation for discharge current 1.0 ​A. (c)
V batt
vs
O C V
relation for discharge current 1.5 ​A. (d)
V batt
vs
O C V
relation for discharge current 2.0 ​A.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Consequently, it is observed that the inclusion of PCA, even for a dataset with four variables, leads to a notable decrease in the RMSE value.

row.entry: Based on the developed experimental prototype comprising of customized battery pack
UDDS Drive Cycle based on Ref. [29]
Standard cell-lvel data based on Ref. [32]

head.ce:title: Optimized ANN for LiFePO4 battery charge estimation using principal components based feature generation

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Computer Science and Engineering, School of Technology, Pandit Deendayal Energy University, Raysan, Gandhinagar 382007, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Computer Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Electrical Engineering, School of Energy Technology, Pandit Deendayal Energy University, Raysan, Gandhinagar 382007, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Electrical Engineering, School of Energy Technology, Pandit Deendayal Energy University, Raysan, Gandhinagar 382007, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Information and Communication Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Information and Communication Technology, School of Technology, Pandit Deendayal Energy University, Raysan, Gandhinagar 382007, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Electric vehicles (EVs) have gained prominence in the present energy transition scenario. Widespread adoption of EVs necessitates an accurate State of Charge estimation (SoC) algorithm. Integrating predictive SoC estimations with smart charging strategies not only optimizes charging efficiency and grid reliability but also extends battery lifespan while continuously enhancing the accuracy of SoC predictions, marking a crucial milestone in sustainable electric vehicle technology. In this research study, machine learning methods, particularly Artificial Neural Networks (ANN), are employed for SoC estimation of LiFePO4 batteries, resulting in efficient and accurate estimation algorithms. The investigation first focuses on developing a custom-designed battery pack with 12 ​V, 4 Ah capacity with a facility for real-time data collection through a dedicated hardware setup. The voltage, current and open-circuit voltage of the battery are monitored with computerized battery analyzer. The battery temperature is sensed with a DHT22 temperature sensor interfaced with Raspberry Pi. Principal components are derived for the collected battery data set and analyzed for feature engineering. Three principal components were generated as input parameters for the developed ANN. Early Stopping for the ANN was also implemented to achieve faster convergence of the ANN. While considering eleven combinations for ten different optimizers loss function is minimized. Comparative analysis of hyperparameter tuning and optimizer selection revealed that the Adafactor optimizer with specific settings produced the best results with an RMSE value of 0.4083 and an R2 Score of 0.9998. The proposed algorithm was also implemented for two different types of datasets, a UDDS drive cycle and a standard cell-level dataset. The results obtained were in line with the results obtained with the ANN model developed based on the data collected from the developed experimental setup.

ce:section.ce:para: With the wider acceptance of distributed generation, energy storage systems (ESS) play a crucial role in modern power systems for ensuring grid stability, enhancing renewable energy integration, optimizing load management, mitigating peak demand, providing backup power, and fostering overall energy resiliency [1]. Among various energy storage technologies, batteries have emerged as the most promising technology for ESS applications. Battery energy storage (BES) is universally employed as an energy source for electric vehicles (EVs). The renewable energy-based charging of EV batteries significantly impacts reducing carbon emissions and mitigating climate change. Hence, owing to their low carbon emissions and reduced dependence on fossil fuels EVs have become a promising alternative to conventional internal combustion engine (ICE) vehicles.

The battery pack in EVs is the primary source of energy. The battery's safe operation is a crucial functionality of the Battery Management System (BMS). It helps ensure safe and efficient battery and subsequent vehicle operation. BMS monitors and controls the battery pack's operating conditions, such as temperature, voltage, and current, and performs State of Charge (SoC) estimation to determine the remaining capacity of the battery and State of Health (SoH) to determine the remaining life of the battery [2]. Moreover, BMS ensures cell balancing in the pack and protects the battery from overcharging, deep discharging, and overheating. Therefore, BMS is a critical component in EVs and is crucial in battery safety, performance, and lifespan [3]. Inaccurate SoC estimation can lead to poor battery performance, failure in estimating the remaining driving range, premature battery failure, and potential safety hazards [4]. Therefore, accurate SoC estimation is crucial to the performance and safety of EVs. Smart charging is an adaptive and intelligent approach to electric vehicle charging that optimizes the process based on grid capacity, energy cost, and renewable energy availability, ensuring efficient resource utilization and grid stability [5]. By aligning charging patterns with predictive SoC estimations, it optimizes charging times, reducing strain on the grid during peak demand while ensuring EVs are sufficiently charged for upcoming journeys. This synergy not only enhances grid stability but also prolongs battery life by avoiding unnecessary charging cycles. Additionally, by leveraging smart charging insights, battery SoC estimation algorithms can refine their predictions, creating a feedback loop that enhances the accuracy of SoC estimates over time. This symbiotic relationship between smart charging strategies and SoC estimations represents a pivotal advancement in the realm of electric vehicle technologies, fostering efficient energy use and sustainable transportation practices [6].

Richter et al. presented a survey on thermal conductivity and internal temperature profiling in Li-ion secondary batteries. The significance of accurate thermal characterization for the battery's safety, performance and lifespan is highlighted. Challenges and limitations of existing approaches are also discussed. Valuable insights into the thermal management of Li-ion batteries are also provided [7]. Hence, SoC estimation without due consideration to battery temperature may not yield accurate results.

SoC estimation is very challenging due to the complex battery chemistry, nonlinear battery characteristics, effects of temperature and current on battery performance, etc. Different SoC estimation methods, such as model-based, observer-based, and data-driven approaches, are reported in the literature [2,3,8–11]. Model-based approaches employ mathematical models of batteries, particularly equivalent circuit models (ECM). The model parameters are designed using the voltage and current characteristics of the battery [12]. Additionally, based on the impact of the temperature on the voltage and current characteristics is also incorporated in the model. The model's accuracy and, consequently, the SoC estimation suffers if inaccurate model parameters are used [8]. Often, it is difficult to obtain the model parameters accurately owing to the battery's nonlinear behaviour [13]. Observer-based approaches offer an alternate method for SoC estimation. These methods use Kalman filters or similar approaches to estimate SoC based on the voltage, current, and temperature of the battery. These approaches can be more accurate than model-based ones, but they require high computational complexity and can be challenging to implement in real-time applications. Using observer-based methods for SoC estimation comes with several notable drawbacks, including complexity, sensitivity to model mismatch, and challenges in initialization. These limitations underscore the need for careful consideration when selecting SoC estimation methods for specific applications [14–16].

Data-driven approaches, such as machine learning (ML) algorithms, have transpired as promising alternatives for SoC estimation in EVs. ML algorithms, such as artificial neural networks (ANN), can learn and recognize complex data patterns and estimate SoC based on the battery parameters. ANN algorithms are reported to considerably improve the accuracy of SoC estimation [17]. ANN-based SoC estimation requires relevant input features that capture the critical information essential for accurate SoC estimation. However, selecting relevant input features is challenging, and it can significantly affect the accuracy of SoC estimation. Additionally, the optimizer implemented to minimize the error between the predicted and actual value can substantially impact the accuracy of the ANN model. The selection of optimizer type for improving the accuracy of the ANN model is found to be less explored to the best knowledge of the authors. Several optimizers such as stochastic gradient descent (SGD), Adam, RMSprop, Adagrad, AdamW, Adadelta, Adamax, Adafactor, Nadam and Ftrl, can be utilized for ANN training. However, the performance of these optimizers can vary significantly depending on the characteristics of the data and the ANN model [9]. Therefore, evaluating the performance of different optimizers for the ANN-based battery SoC estimation problem is essential.

Thus, the existing SoC estimation methods have shortcomings that hinder their robustness, accuracy, or pose challenges in real-time implementation. Some methods rely on simple voltage or current-based approaches, neglecting the influence of battery temperature and ageing effects. Others depend on complex physics-based models, demanding extensive parameter identification and computational resources. Addressing these limitations is crucial to developing more reliable and practical SoC estimation methods for effective battery management and electric vehicle applications [11].

Misyris et al. in Ref. [12], comprehensively analyzed the SoC estimation techniques for Li-ion batteries. The paper reviews existing research in the field, identifies limitations in current approaches, and introduces a novel hybrid method that combines model-based and data-driven techniques. Kuchly et al. [18], have discussed using ANN and integrating a physics-based Porous Electrode Theory and Diffusion (P2D) model for generating training data. The paper critically evaluates the existing methods, identifies research gaps, and proposes a novel approach that leverages a neural network trained with P2D model-generated data for accurate SoC estimation in Li-ion batteries. The P2D method used by the author can be computationally intensive and require extensive parameterization, making it challenging to implement for real-time applications.

PCA is used to analyze the degradation of battery health by Banguero et al. in Ref. [19]. The paper discusses various approaches, methodologies, and case studies and provides insights into the effectiveness of PCA-based diagnosis techniques for battery energy storage systems. The novelty of this paper lies in the integration of PCA for feature generation in battery SoC estimation, thereby addressing the limitations of traditional approaches. The RMSE value for the developed ANN without PCA is 0.5674. Implementing SoC estimation while considering the additional input parameters for feature generation would be very interesting.

The paper proposes the use of principal components-based feature generation and optimized ANN for SoC estimation in LiFePO4. The use of PCA results in enhanced accuracy and interpretability of ANN model of SoC estimation. The data is collected using an experimental setup with four different discharge currents. PCA determines the most informative features from diverse battery performance parameters namely temperature, current, voltage and open-circuit voltage. Ten different optimizers (SGD, Adam, RMSprop, Adagrad, AdamW, Adadelta, Adamax, Adafactor, Nadam and Ftrl) are used for training to further improve the accuracy of the designed ANN model. Google Colab platform has been used as an IDE and for the required libraries. Additionally, for each optimizer eleven different combinations of hyperparameters are utilized to increase the overall accuracy of SoC estimation. A detailed comparison of SoC estimation with different optimizers and hyperparameters is carried out. Considering RMSE and R2 Score as the evaluating parameters, the comparative analysis indicates that Adafactor optimizer results in the most accurate ANN model for the SoC estimation in LiFePO4 battery. To summarize, the three novelties incorporated in this paper are (a)
O C V
and
T batt
are added in the input feature set; (b) PCA is used to generate features; and (c) the best-performing optimizer is selected for the ANN algorithm.

The study focuses on the 18,650-lithium iron phosphate cell (18,650 LiFePO4 cell) due to its widespread use in electric vehicles, offering superior thermal conductivity compared to other chemistries [20]. An experimental setup, comprising of eight 18,650 LiFePO4 cells, a computerized battery analyzer, a DHT22 temperature sensor, Raspberry Pi 4, was developed for data collection. Series connections of four 18,650 LiFePO4 cells were made. Two such series connections of four LiFePO4 cells were connected in parallel to develop a custom-designed 12 ​V, 4 Ah battery pack. With a computerized battery analyzer, battery current and terminal voltage of the battery were recorded at the sample rate of 1 sample/second. Discharge tests were conducted using a computerized battery analyzer (CBA) capable of discharging the battery pack at a constant current. The battery was discharged at four different discharge rates for the purpose of data collection. Unlike conventional battery testers, CBAs enable efficient and realistic testing of batteries under real-world conditions. Additionally, after every 30 ​min the experimentation was paused and open-circuit voltage of the battery was measured.

As the temperature distribution varies across the battery pack, the DHT22 temperature sensor were connected at three locations, namely at the first, middle, and last cell of the battery pack. This approach allowed for a comprehensive understanding of temperature variations within the battery. Additionally, incorporating temperature variations at various locations into the battery model can enhance the accuracy of the estimation algorithm. The temperature directly impacts the chemical reactions occurring within the battery pack, consequently influencing its capacity [21]. Data from the temperature sensor was collected through a Raspberry Pi 4, featuring a 1.2 ​GHz 64-bit quad-core processor, 4 ​GB RAM, and ARMv8 CPU. The Raspberry Pi platform offers seamless multitasking capabilities and online connectivity, making it an excellent choice for data collection.

As mentioned earlier, the
O C V
was determined by pausing the discharge cycle at 30-min intervals to measure the value of
O C V
at regular intervals during each discharge cycle. This method aided in determining the
O C V
values for the dataset. A relation was identified between the
V batt
and
O C V
as shown in Fig. 1 , for the collected dataset for all the discharge cycles.

The initial SoC value holds critical importance for the accurate calculation of the initial range of the vehicle. The
O C V
values assist in determining the SoC of the battery at the beginning of the prediction process. Another parameter that affects battery's chemical reaction is the battery temperature (
T batt
).
T batt
affects the battery's capacity, charge–discharge cycle, and ultimately the SoC. Hence, temperature serves as a significant parameter that needs to be incorporated into the SoC estimation process. The research facilitates the use of all four parameters,
V batt
,
I batt
,
O C V
and
T batt
as the inputs to the PCA.

Feature engineering is the process of selecting and transforming raw data into meaningful and informative features that enhance the performance of machine learning models [22]. In this paper, feature engineering plays a vital role in cleaning the data collected during the charge–discharge cycle of LiFePO4 battery. Once the feature engineering is performed, it is observed that the resulting data is expected to increase accuracy and a comprehensive understanding of battery performance. In general, batteries exhibit intricate behavior influenced by multiple factors, including voltage, current, temperature, and time. The process of feature engineering involves transforming raw battery data into meaningful and informative features that effectively capture the underlying patterns and characteristics. By carefully selecting and engineering these features, the accuracy, reliability, and interpretability of battery parameters can be enhanced for estimation algorithms. This, in turn, facilitates a deeper understanding, improved monitoring, and effective control of battery systems across various applications, such as electric vehicles, renewable energy storage, portable electronic devices etc.

PCA is a dimensionality reduction technique that enables the extraction of essential information from high-dimensional data. PCA holds significant importance in feature engineering for various research applications. By transforming the original features into a new set of uncorrelated variables, called principal components, PCA effectively captures the most relevant information while reducing data redundancy [23].

In the study, various parameters considered for SoC estimation of the LiFePO4 battery are
I batt
,
V batt
,
T batt
and
O C V
. The correlation between datasets can be observed through the help of Heatmaps. Heatmaps are used as a tool for data visualization and exploratory data analysis. They are particularly useful when dealing with large datasets and when there is a need for quick identification of patterns and correlations between variables. After collecting the data and performing feature engineering on the dataset, selecting an algorithm that will effectively and accurately estimate the SoC of the battery is essential. Data-driven, machine learning techniques have gained prominence as a noteworthy area of investigation in accurately estimating the SoC of batteries.

ANN is a parallel processing unit that can describe complex and nonlinear behavior by using input–output relationships in the datasets [24]. In ANN, information is processed through the network by transmitting signals between neurons. Each connection between neurons has an associated weight that determines the strength of the signal. Neurons apply an activation function to the weighted sum of their inputs, allowing them to introduce non-linearity into the network [25]. LiFePO4 batteries are highly nonlinear and their operating characteristics are affected by temperature, ageing, C-rate, etc. ANN is most suitable for modelling the highly dynamic and nonlinear nature of LiFePO4 batteries [10]. ANN is an adaptive system that changes the neuron structure, its weights and connections depending on the input dataset used for training the algorithm. The accuracy of the ANN can be increased by using a large dataset with varied operating conditions. The drawback of using a large dataset is that the execution time, training time and computational complexity increase significantly. A large dataset of the charge–discharge cycle of LiFePO4 batteries is required for using ANN for battery SoC estimation. Fig. 2 shows the ANN used in the study with one input, three hidden, and one output layer. Feed-Forward (FF) ANN with Back Propagation based learning is used for the study. The iterative training process in FFANN allows the model to adapt and optimize its parameters, providing a robust framework for accurate SoC predictions in diverse operational conditions. The FFANN emerges as a robust choice for SoC estimation, as the method leverages its capacity to discern intricate patterns and nonlinear dependencies in battery dynamics [26]. The adaptability and optimization capabilities of FF ANN, as outlined by Zhang et al. in [27], make it a compelling model for accurate and reliable SoC predictions, especially when confronted with the varied and dynamic nature of real-world operating conditions. The three hidden layers in the ANN contain sixteen, thirty-two and sixty-four neurons, respectively. The number of input neurons is three, as three principal components are considered as input parameters. The output layer contains a single neuron for predicting the SoC of the battery. The activation function used in the neurons, spread across the different layers, is ReLU. One primary advantage is its ability to mitigate the vanishing gradient problem. ANN rely on gradient descent-based optimization methods, which can lead to slow convergence or even stagnation when gradients become extremely small. Traditional activation functions like sigmoid and tanh can saturate for large inputs, causing gradient vanishing. However, ReLU does not saturate in the positive range, allowing gradients to flow during backpropagation, thus facilitating faster training and improved convergence [28].

The paper presents PCA-based feature engineering and ANN is used for the estimation of SoC for a LiFePO4 battery. A 12 ​V, 4Ah battery pack was designed for this purpose. 18,650 LiFePO4 cells were used as this cell chemistry is widely used in EVs owing to its higher thermal tolerance [7]. The data was collected through a dedicated hardware setup DHT22 thermal sensor, CBA and Raspberry Pi. The CBA is capable of providing a constant discharge current. For the study, the battery pack underwent discharge utilizing various constant discharge currents, viz 0.5 ​A, 1 ​A, 1.5 ​A, and 2 ​A. The parameters collected during the data collection part were
I batt
,
V batt
,
T batt
and
O C V
. These parameters were considered as input parameters to the estimation algorithm and the output parameter was SoC of the battery.

In the study, the data collected through CBA and Raspberry Pi was at a frequency interval of 1 data point per second. The dataset amounts to (59,813 ​× ​3) input data points. The image of the hardware setup developed for data collection is shown in Fig. 3 . The DHT22 temperature sensor was physically deployed on the surface of the custom-designed 12 ​V, 4 Ah battery at various locations, viz the first cell, the middle cell and the last cell. The sensor has been secured using a mechanical clamp on the cell. It is further tightened using insulated tape so that the sensor is always in contact with the cells of the battery. This strategy was adopted to incorporate the varying effect of temperature on the battery state estimation. Both Raspberry Pi and CBA were connected to a system where data was being recorded.

Following are the steps followed for the study.
Algorithm Steps for the Study:
S-1: Gathering the input data by utilizing the hardware setup
S-2: Data cleaning
S-3: Plotting heatmap to visualize the correlation between input parameters
S-4: Feature extraction using PCA
S-5: Plotting heatmap for the newly generated principal components to visualize their correlation
S-6: Dividing the data into 80%-20% for train-test split
S-7: Utilizing the training data to train the ANN
S-8: Analyzing the effect of ten different optimizers and hyperparameter tuning of optimizers with eleven conditions on the training of ANN
S-9: Select the best performing optimizer and hyperparameter set for testing the data based on the RMSE and R2 score value
S-10: Plotting the actual vs predicted SoC graph

The first step was to gather input data with the help of hardware setup. The collected data had missing values; hence data cleaning was performed on the collected dataset. This increased the readability and operability of the data. Data cleaning is a critical and indispensable step in the research process, particularly when dealing with large and complex datasets. Data cleaning involves a series of tasks, including handling missing values through imputation methods, identifying and correcting data entry errors, and detecting outliers. By removing or correcting erroneous data points, data cleaning enhances the integrity of the dataset, allowing researchers to draw accurate conclusions and make informed decisions [23]. For measuring OCV during short disconnection periods, it is proposed to measure the OCV when the vehicle is turned-off. If the off time exceeds 10 ​min, a new OCV measurement will be taken to ensure accuracy. However, for shorter off durations, the previous stable OCV value will be utilized in the SOC calculation. We believe this strategy balances the need for real-time SOC estimation with the requirement for stable OCV data.

In the collected dataset, there were missing values for the feature
O C V
. The missing values were replaced with the mean value of the
O C V
. Fig. 4 shows the flow diagram for the simulation study undertaken in this paper.

To understand the correlation between features, heatmap was plotted. A heatmap is a graphical representation of a matrix where the values in each cell are represented by colors. In a heatmap, the rows and columns of the matrix represent the features or variables, and the values in each cell represent the correlation between the two features. High correlations are represented by warm colors like blue, while low correlations are represented by cool colors like off-white or teal. Fig. 5 represents the Heatmap of the input features used for the simulation in the study. From the plotted Heatmap, it is evident that the relation between
O C V
and
V batt
is the same. This indicates that omitting any one of the two features will not have any impact on the estimation of the SoC of the battery.

However, the inclusion of
O C V
for the estimation algorithm is critical. The
O C V
value is used to estimate the initial SoC of the battery when the battery is not fully charged at the start of the discharge cycle. Principal Component Analysis (PCA) method is a feature engineering method used to transform information from features to newly generated principal components. The use of this method increases the interpretability of data while preserving the maximum amount of information from the data. In the simulation study, PCA was used on features O C V ,
V batt
,
I batt
and
T batt
and three new features PC 1, PC 2 and PC 3 were generated. Thus, creating a feature set of (59,813 ​× ​3) input data points.

After the feature set is generated, the dataset is split into the train-test dataset. The data was split in an 80–20 ratio. 80% dataset was used to train the algorithm and the remaining 20% of the dataset was used to test the algorithm. The data-driven ANN algorithm was employed for the estimation of SoC for LiFePO4 batteries. There are various parameters like the number of neurons, number of hidden layers, optimizers selection, etc. that affect the accuracy of the ANN. The study focuses on the selection of the best-performing optimizer for the estimation algorithm.

The selection of an appropriate optimizer plays a crucial role in effectively training ANN. Optimizers are algorithms that adjust the weights and biases of the neural network during the learning process to minimize the error or loss function [24]. Different optimizers have unique characteristics and convergence behaviors, making the choice of optimizer critical for achieving optimal performance. Ten different optimizers used in the study are stochastic gradient descent (SGD), Adam, RMSprop, Adagrad, AdamW, Adadelta, Adamax, Adafactor, Nadam and Ftrl.

The algorithm converged through the early sopping method on root mean square error (RMSE). Early stopping is a technique used to prevent overfitting and improve algorithm convergence. It involves monitoring a validation metric, such as RMSE, during training and stopping the training process when the validation metric no longer improves or starts to degrade. By stopping the training at the point of optimal validation performance, early stopping helps to avoid overfitting and ensures that the model generalizes well to unseen data.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance analysis of the proposed algorithm with the data collected from the developed experimental setup
ce:section.ce:para:
In the available literature, the focus was extensively on the improvement of the estimation algorithm. The focus of this paper is not only to increase the algorithm efficiency but also to select additional features for the estimation of SoC. This, in turn, increases the reliability and robustness of the algorithm. There are three novelties incorporated in the paper, a)
O C V
and
T batt
are added in the input feature set; b) PCA is used to generate features; and c) the best-performing optimizer is selected for the ANN algorithm.

The first step is the collection of data for training the algorithm. A hardware setup was designed for data collection, and then the data was used for the simulation study for the estimation of SoC for a LiFePO4 battery pack. A 12 ​V, 4Ah battery pack was designed for the study. The data was collected in two parts: (a) part–1:
V batt
−
I batt
and
O C V
measured through a CBA; and (b) part–2: battery temperature
T batt
was measured through connecting a DHT22 temperature sensor to the battery and Raspberry pi. The sampling rate on both devices was one sample per second. Within the existing literature study undertaken, it has been noted that while integrating temperature as an input parameter into ANN simulations, the focus tends to be on ambient temperature alone, with an omission of the temperature variations among distinct cells.

After the data was collected, feature engineering step was performed which included, data cleaning and replacing missing values of the dataset, and a heatmap of the dataset was plotted. Through the heatmap, it was evident that there is a direct relation between
V batt
and
O C V
. This meant that even if one of the parameters was omitted, there would be little to no effect on the result of estimation of SoC. However, incorporating both the parameters was essential as both
V batt
and
O C V
reveal intricate battery behavior. PCA was also used during the feature engineering step for the generation of new features.

The Explained Covariance graph is plotted for the principal components as shown in Fig. 6 . The graph provides insights into the amount of variance in the data that is accounted for by each principal component. It plots the percentage of the total variance in the dataset explained by the corresponding number of principal components. The initial steepness of the bar indicates the extent to which the first principal components capture the dominant patterns or structures in the data. A steeper slope suggests that a smaller number of components can explain a significant portion of the variance, indicating strong underlying patterns. The vertical axis indicates the cumulative explained variance. Higher values indicate that the principal components considered so far account for a larger portion of the total variance in the dataset [22].

The output Explained Variance: [0.789,381,07, 0.171,382,59, 0.039,236,35] indicates that the first principal component explains approximately 78.94% of the variance in the dataset, followed by the second component explaining around 17.14%, and the third component explaining about 3.92%. This suggests that the first principal component captures the most significant patterns and structures in the data, followed by diminishing contributions from the subsequent components.

The heatmap assisted in realizing the correlation between all the input parameters. The next step was to implement the ANN for the estimation of the SoC of the battery with the newly generated feature set. The ANN model was developed with three hidden layers each layer with sixteen, thirty-two and sixty-four neurons, respectively. One of the significant parameters for the ANN to perform optimally is the selection of the optimizer used to optimize the model's parameters such as adjusting the weights and biases of the neural network based on the calculated gradients during the training process, so as to minimize the error or loss function.

The paper investigates the effects of different optimizers for the developed ANN to estimate the SoC of the LiFePO4 battery. Various optimizers used for the study are SGD, Adam, RMSprop, Adagrad, AdamW, Adadelta, Adamax, Adafactor, Nadam and Ftrl. The study involved conducting analyses with varying parameter selections, specifically focusing on momentum, learning rate, and clip value adjustments. These parameters were systematically altered to explore their impact on the research outcomes. The results were compared on the basis of RMSE value and R2 Score. The presented table illustrates the analysis of results obtained using various focus parameters for all identified optimizers. The table showcases the performance of each optimizer under different parameter settings, providing valuable insights into their effectiveness and suitability for the research study.

Table 1 consists of all the conditions for the focus parameters used for the simulation. As a part of the research work, a total of 11 combinations, mentioned in Table 1, were used for each optimizer. The clip value is a parameter used to prevent the gradients from becoming too large during the training process of the ANN and the value can be set to yes or no. Gradients represent the rate at which the weights of the network need to be adjusted to minimize the loss function during training. If the gradients become too large, it can lead to unstable training and hinder the convergence of the model. The clip norm sets a maximum norm for the gradients, ensuring they do not exceed the specified value. Clip value directly limits the gradients' magnitude, preventing them from becoming too large or unstable during the learning process. For clip norm, values are usually set in the range of 1–5. A value of 1 implies that the gradients will be scaled to have a maximum Euclidean norm of 1, ensuring that no gradient surpasses this norm during training. For clip value, typical values range from 0.1 to 1.0. Lower clip values restrict the gradients more aggressively, while higher values allow for slightly larger gradients.

The next hyperparameters considered for tuning are the learning rate and momentum for an ANN. The learning rate is a critical hyperparameter in tuning ANN during training. It determines the step size by which the model's weights are updated based on the calculated gradients. An optimal learning rate is essential for achieving faster convergence and preventing divergence or overshooting during training. A higher value for the learning rate results in unstable learning and hinders the convergence of the algorithm, whereas a lower value for the learning rate can lead to slow training and the algorithm getting stuck in local minima. By tuning the momentum, the optimizer accumulates past gradients, giving a continuous push to the current gradients, allowing the model to escape shallow local minima and reach the global minimum. Momentum plays a vital role in speeding up training, particularly in deep or complex networks and enhances the ANN's ability to generalize well on unseen data.

Table 2 represents the comparative analysis of RMSE value for all the identified optimizers evaluated and compared for the research. Table 3 represents the comparative analysis of the R2 Score value for the same identified optimizers. For easier interpretation of trends and patterns in data, Figs. 8 and 9 depicts the graphical representation of the RMSE and R2 Score values for all the identified conditions for better visualization. For example, in the results for condition C, where the clip norm and clip values are zero, the learning rate is 0.1, and the momentum is 0.99. The error is higher for all the optimizers as the higher learning rate leads to an increased step size during optimization, making it more likely to overshoot the minimum of the loss function. This can result in the algorithm failing to converge, increasing error or oscillating around the minimum. All ten identified optimizers have been tested for conditions A through K, as itemized in Table 1. The RMSE and R2 Score are considered as evaluating parameters as RMSE quantifies the average prediction error by measuring the difference between the predicted and the actual values. The lower the RMSE value, the more accurate the model is, making it more suitable for the estimation algorithm. R2 Score assesses the variance proportion in the target variable which the model tries to explain, a higher value indicates a better model fit. R2 Score assists in understanding how well the ANN captures patterns in the data.

The ANN developed for the research is observed to perform best with the Adafactor optimizer. The optimizer required gradient clipping, thus setting the clip value to 1.0 and the clip norm to 1.0. This results in reduced RMSE and a higher R2 Score. By using different values for learning rate and momentum, it was observed that the algorithm performed best with the learning rate value set to 0.01 and momentum set to 0.8. A lower rate of 0.01, offers stability and prevents overshooting of the ANN training, albeit potentially requiring more training iterations. The observed momentum value signifies a notable influence of prior gradient information. High momentum values prioritize recent gradient history, aiding escape from local minima and hastening convergence. However, excessive momentum can lead to instability. Here, the 0.8 momentum value demonstrates a desire to leverage momentum's benefits while cautiously preventing undue turbulence. These hyperparameters are evidently well-suited for the estimation algorithm, given the notable RMSE and R2 Score values. The outcome of the comparative analysis is that ANN performs best with Adafactor optimizer and hyperparameter tuning as per condition J. For this combination of optimizer and hyperparameter values, the RMSE observed was 0.4083 and the R2 Score was 0.9998, which was the best performance among all the other values. The predicted vs actual SoC value is in Fig. 7
.

As evidenced in previous studies, PCA has not conventionally been applied for SoC estimation. While PCA is typically employed for large datasets to enhance data interpretability by reducing dimensions, this paper demonstrates a novel application of PCA in SoC simulation, showcasing a reduction in RMSE. When employing the most optimal optimizer, Adafactor, and a well-performing set of hyperparameters condition J, the comparison of results is revealed as indicated in Table 4 below.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance analysis of the proposed algorithm with the standard UDDS drive cycle
ce:section.ce:para: The UDDS (Urban Dynamometer Driving Schedule) drive cycle is a standardized test cycle used to simulate urban driving conditions. It mimics a typical city driving pattern by involving frequent stops, starts and changes in speed. The variation in vehicle speed provides a challenge for SoC estimation algorithms due to sudden changes in current. The importance of evaluating the proposed SoC estimation algorithm on the UDDS drive cycle lies in its representativeness of real-world urban driving scenarios.

The UDDS drive cycle dataset, provided by [29], comprises of battery current
C batt
. Based on this dataset, the 18,650 Li-ion battery model (7.2 ​V, 5.4 Ah) was developed in MATLAB/SIMULINK. The battery model was subjected to UDDS drive cycle. The collected dataset was
I batt
,
V batt
and
S o C
. This is as per the step-by-step procedure for generating the dataset for the UDDS drive cycle as given in [30]. The initial SoC was considered to be 100%. For simulating the UDDS drive cycle in MATLAB/SIMULINK, the battery model was developed using a constant current source and signal builder. Input to the signal builder was
C batt
, which is obtained from dataset given in Ref. [29]. The output of the signal builder was given to a constant current source, which acted as a discharge current for the battery. Scope was used for displaying and recording the output variables,
I batt
,
V batt
and
S o C
, of the battery during the discharge cycle.

The dataset comprising of
I batt
,
V batt
and
S o C
, obtained as output from the MATLAB/SIMULINK model of the battery were used as an input to the proposed PCA and ANN-based SoC estimation. For the optimizer study, different optimizers such as stochastic gradient descent (SGD), Adam, RMSprop, Adagrad, AdamW, Adadelta, Adamax, Adafactor, Nadam and Ftrl, were utilized for training the ANN. All the optimizers were trained using eleven different conditions as outlined in Table 1. The outcome of the study revealed that the hyperparameter values presented in Condition J (Clip value as 0, Learning Rate value as 0.01 and Momentum value as 0.8) provided the best result. This finding is in line with the results obtained with the ANN model developed based on the data collected from the developed experimental setup discussed in Section 3. Performance analysis of the proposed algorithm with the standard UDDS drive cycle reveals the respective RMSE and R2 Score for Condition J to be 0.430,7 and 0.999,7. Table 5 illustrates a comparative examination of RMSE values for all assessed and compared optimizers in the study. Likewise, Table 6 demonstrates a comparative analysis of R2 Score values for the same optimizers identified.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance analysis of the proposed algorithm with the dataset for standard cell-level dataset
ce:section.ce:para:
Reis et al. in Ref. [31] have done a comprehensive study and summarized the dataset for Li-ion available in the public domain. Based on Ref. [31], the dataset for the standard 18,650 Li-ion cell was obtained from [32]. This dataset was found to be very much suitable as along with
I batt
and
V batt
, battery temperature,
T batt
, is also included. This dataset was used to evaluate the performance of proposed PCA and ANN-based SoC estimation. In line with the performance analysis carried out in this paper, for the optimizer study, different optimizers such as stochastic gradient descent (SGD), Adam, RMSprop, Adagrad, AdamW, Adadelta, Adamax, Adafactor, Nadam and Ftrl, were utilized for training the ANN model. All the optimizers were trained utilizing eleven different conditions as outlined in Table 1.

The outcome of the study revealed that the hyperparameter values presented in Condition J (Clip value as 0, Learning Rate value as 0.01 and Momentum value as 0.8) gave the best result. This finding aligns with the outcomes derived from the ANN model developed using data from the developed experimental setup detailed in Section 3. Performance analysis of the proposed algorithm with the standard cell-level dataset yields the respective RMSE and R2 Score for Condition J to be 0.408,9 and 0.999,8. Table 7 represents the comparative analysis of RMSE values for all the identified optimizers evaluated and compared for the research. Similarly, Table 8 represents the comparative analysis of the R2 Score value for the same identified optimizers.

In summary, the optimizer analysis study performed on all three datasets gave similar results for hyperparameter values. Table 9 summarises the difference in RMSE values and R2 Sciore values for all three datasets.

Integrating precise SoC estimation with intelligent mart chargig methods significantly improves grid stability, charging efficiency and battery longevity, representing a substantial leap forward in sustainable electric vehicle technology. The widespread penetration of on-road EVs necessitates precise SoC estimation. The research presented in this paper, investigates the use of machine learning techniques, with a specific focus on ANN, to develop efficient and accurate SoC estimation methods for LiFePO4 batteries. A custom-designed battery pack of 12 ​V, 4 Ah capacity was developed as a part of the research using LiFePO4 cells. The data was collected by developing a hardware setup using a CBA for
V batt
,
I batt
and
O C V
and DHT22 temperature sensor integrated with Raspberry Pi 4 for
T batt
.

Feature engineering was performed on the collected dataset. The heatmap analysis implicated that the effects of
V batt
and
O C V
are the same and either parameter can be excluded without much effect on the estimation of the SoC of the battery. However, it is necessary to include the effect of both these parameters for the estimation algorithm training, as the battery SoC when the battery is not fully charged can be estimated using
O C V
. Three principal components were obtained by using PCA which included the effect of
V batt
,
I batt
,
T batt
and
O C V
. After the new dataset was obtained training of ANN started. Comparative analysis of hyperparameter tuning and optimizer selection was performed. Eleven conditions were studied on ten optimizers with different hyperparameter combinations, such as clip value, clip norm, learning rate and momentum. The evaluation parameters used for the study were RMSE and R2 Score. The findings revealed that using Adafactor optimizer with a clip value set to 0, clip norm to 0, learning rate set to 0.01 and momentum value set to 0.8 yielded the optimal performance for the algorithm. This configuration resulted in an RMSE value of 0.408,3 and an R2 Score of 0.999,8. The same approach was applied to two distinct datasets, namely a UDDS drive cycle and a standard cell-level dataset. For the UDDS drive cycle, the respective RMSE and R2 Score for Condition J are to be 0.430,7 and 0.999,7. Similarly, for standard cell-level datasets, the respective values for RMSE and R2 Score for Condition J are 0.408,9 and 0.999,8. These outcomes are consistent with those achieved using the ANN model trained on data gathered from the established experimental setup.
Chaitali Mehta: Conceptualization, Visualization, Execution, Methodology, Data Curation, Original Draft Preparation.
Amit V. Sant: Conceptualization, Supervision, Methodology, Validation, Draft Paper Reviewing and Editing.
Paawan Sharma: Conceptualization, Methodology, Supervision, Draft Paper Reviewing and Editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
